$description: Composition patterns for reading surfaces — the primary experience of the portal. A reading surface is where Yogananda's words meet the seeker's eyes. Every detail serves sustained, contemplative reading.

chapter-reader.$description: The full chapter reading experience. The most important surface in the entire ecosystem.

tokens.border: none — reading surfaces float, they don't contain

typography.epigraph: srf.typography.reading-light, italic, centered, gold quotation mark

modes.focus: Chrome hidden. Reading column only. Next chapter nav preserved.
modes.present: Chrome hidden. Font size scaled up (24px mobile → 36px wide). For group reading.
modes.quiet: Chrome hidden. Article dimmed. Contextual quiet corner overlay.

features.dwell: 1200ms settled paragraph → related teachings panel. Gold subliminal background on active paragraph.
features.keyboard: j/k paragraph navigation, h/l chapter, d dwell toggle, f focus mode, ? help
features.scroll-indicator: 2px gold bar at top, opacity ambient (0.3), z-index top
features.keyboard-design-stance: Vim philosophy — discoverable via ?, invisible otherwise. Keyboard shortcuts never appear in the UI unprompted. The core gesture vocabulary (click, scroll, type) handles all essential tasks. Source: DES-006.

practice-bridge.$description: Visual treatment for editorial annotations where Yogananda explicitly invites the reader from reading to practice. Not every mention of meditation — only passages where the author's intent is clearly 'do this.' The design system defines how this looks; the portal owns the specific copy and URLs. Source: DES-006, ADR-104.
practice-bridge.placement: Below citation, after the passage that invites practice. Same vertical position as a 'Find this book' bookstore link.

visual-treatment.typography: reading-light weight, text-sm size. The quietest typographic voice on the reading surface.
visual-treatment.spacing: Standard paragraph spacing above. No extra visual separation — part of the passage's orbit, not a separate card.
visual-treatment.border: None. No card, no box, no background. Just text at a whisper.

practice-bridge.design-stance: Not a modal, not a card, not a CTA. A signpost, not a funnel. Same visual weight as a bookstore link. The reader who is ready will see it; the reader who is reading will not be interrupted.

publication-overlay.$description: Publication reading overlay. When the chapter reader displays content from a published SRF/YSS book (AoY, Science of Religion, God Talks with Arjuna, etc.), structural elements shift from theme text color to crimson. Activated via data-publication attribute on the reader container. The portal chrome (gold) remains unchanged — creating two visual layers: the book's structural voice (crimson) and the portal's interactive voice (gold). This mirrors the printed edition's typography where chapter titles and dividers are rubricated (printed in red) while body text is black.

chapter-title.description: Chapter titles render in crimson, matching the printed AoY. The strongest visual signal of 'you are reading the book.'

chapter-label.description: The 'CHAPTER 1' label above the title. Crimson small-caps — exactly as printed.

scene-break.style: Swelled rule — linear gradient tapering from crimson at center to transparent at edges, max-width 5em, centered
scene-break.description: The pause between scenes within a chapter. A swelled rule — the Western bookmaking form where the line thickens at center and tapers to nothing at edges. In publication context, automatically shifts from gold to crimson. Alternative: .motif .motif-lotus .motif-breath for a botanical pause. The AoY Kindle edition uses a similar device between scenes.
scene-break.hierarchy-note: This sits between the full motif-divider (section break) and motif-breath (paragraph pause). See typography.language.json scene-break for the complete pause hierarchy.

drop-cap.description: Chapter-opening initial letter in crimson. The illuminated manuscript tradition — the reader's eye drawn to the beginning. Latin only (ADR-080).

unchanged-elements.body-text: Remains theme.text (navy/cream per theme). Crimson never colors prose.
unchanged-elements.epigraph: Gold decorative quotation mark stays gold — the epigraph is portal ornamentation.
unchanged-elements.footnote-refs: Gold interactive — these are portal navigation, not book structure.

publication-overlay.theme-behavior: Crimson adapts per theme via --color-crimson custom property. Brightens in dark/meditate themes, mutes in sepia/sandstone, warms in night/devotion. Same adaptation pattern as gold.

passage-card.$description: A single passage displayed outside the chapter reader — search results, Today's Wisdom, related teachings panel.
passage-card.register: sacred (the words are still Yogananda's, even in a card)

tokens.border: none (floats on surface color difference) or border.subtle on hover

content.attribution: citation typography — always visible, never truncated (PRI-02)
content.chapter-link: 'Read in context' — UI typography, gold interactive, links to chapter position

states.hover: gold subliminal (0.06) background shift, border appears

passage-card.animation: interaction transition (150ms) on hover/focus. Content transition (300ms) on appear.

todays-wisdom.$description: Daily featured passage on the home page. Single passage at sacred register with generous framing.

tokens.background: theme.bg (same as page — no card boundary)

content.passage: reading typography, slightly larger (display size for short quotes)
content.ornament: small gold lotus divider below attribution (motif system: .motif .motif-lotus .motif-close)

print.$description: Print stylesheet for reading surfaces. Removes all chrome, sets print-appropriate typography.
rules: Hide: header, footer, nav, scroll indicator, dwell icon
Page breaks: never inside passage-card, always before h2 (chapter titles)
External link URLs shown as parenthetical after the link text

commentary-hierarchy.$description: How supplementary content nests around the primary teaching. Named from the Indian commentary tradition — the most sophisticated textual hierarchy in literary history — where layers of interpretation wrap around a root text without obscuring it. The hierarchy governs visual authority when a reading surface presents multiple content levels simultaneously (e.g., Yogananda's words with SRF editorial notes and glossary definitions). Each level has a register, a spatial relationship to the primary text, and a disclosure strategy. The principle: the mūla (root text) is the bindu (still center); everything else orbits it at increasing distance. See aesthetic-theory.language.json for the theoretical framework.
commentary-hierarchy.western-bridge: Comparable to academic critical editions (primary text with footnotes and marginalia), Talmudic page layout (Mishnah at center, Gemara surrounding), or annotated literary editions. The key distinction: commentary serves the text, never competes with it.

mula.meaning: Root text — the guru's verbatim words. The irreducible center.
mula.spatial-relationship: Center. Maximum reading width (42em Latin, 35em Devanagari). Full sacred register treatment.
mula.disclosure: Always visible. Never collapsed, truncated, or summarized. The mūla is the reason the reader is here.

bhashya.meaning: Commentary — interpretive context by an authority close to the source. Illuminates the root text without replacing it.
bhashya.spatial-relationship: Near orbit. Visually distinct from mūla through typography (slightly smaller, different weight or style) and reduced whitespace. Appears inline or in a clearly secondary position — below the passage it illuminates, or in a side panel on wider viewports.
bhashya.disclosure: Visible when contextually relevant. May be toggled or expandable, but never hidden behind multiple interactions. One tap or click maximum.
examples: monastic commentary on a teaching passage

tika.meaning: Annotation — specific clarification of a term, reference, or concept. Serves the reader who wants to go deeper without interrupting the reader who doesn't.
tika.spatial-relationship: Middle orbit. Compact typography (UI font, smaller size). Appears on demand — tooltip, footnote popover, or expandable inline. Never competes with mūla or bhāṣya for vertical space when collapsed.
tika.disclosure: On demand. Triggered by reader action (tap footnote marker, expand glossary term). The annotation exists for the reader who seeks it; it is invisible to the reader who doesn't. Footnote markers use gold interactive for discoverability.

varttika.meaning: Critical supplement — what the text assumes the reader knows. Background knowledge that makes the teaching accessible without annotation-level specificity.
varttika.register: instructional (content is teaching, not UI — but its spatial presence is ambient: available without competing)
varttika.spatial-relationship: Far orbit. Separate from the reading surface entirely — a panel, a linked page, a section below the chapter. Never inline with the teaching.
varttika.disclosure: Available but never promoted. The reader who needs background will find it; the reader who doesn't should never be made aware of it during reading. A discreet 'Background' or 'Context' link, not a banner.
examples: biographical context for people mentioned in the text

nesting-rules: The mūla always receives sacred register treatment regardless of what commentary surrounds it.
Commentary levels never use larger typography than the level above them in the hierarchy.
When bhāṣya and ṭīkā appear on the same surface, their visual distinction must be immediately clear — different font size, weight, or spatial position.
Vārttika never appears inline with the teaching text. It is always spatially separated.
On narrow viewports (< md breakpoint), bhāṣya collapses to expandable sections below the mūla. Ṭīkā becomes footnote-style. Vārttika links to a separate view.
The publication overlay (crimson) applies only to mūla-level structural elements. Commentary always uses gold or theme-text, never crimson.

temporal-rhythm.$description: How the reading surface breathes through time. Based on the prāṇāyāma principle (see aesthetic-theory.language.json): every temporal sequence has three phases — approach (pūraka), hold (kumbhaka), and release (recaka). The held state is the purpose; approach and release serve it. This section maps the principle to specific reading surface transitions.
temporal-rhythm.western-bridge: Film pacing: the establishing shot (approach), the scene (hold), the transition (release). Musical phrasing: anacrusis, tonic, cadence. The key insight the breath model adds: the held state (kumbhaka) is unlimited — the design never rushes the reader out of presence.

chapter-entry.puraka: Content transition (300ms, decelerate easing). The chapter text arrives. The reader approaches the teaching.
chapter-entry.kumbhaka: The reading state. No temporal constraint. No auto-advance, no timer, no nudge. The reader is with the teaching for as long as they choose.
chapter-entry.recaka: Contemplative transition (800ms, accelerate easing). 'Breath Between Chapters' (DES-012). The chapter fades gently. Departure is slower than arrival.

dwell-activation.puraka: Settled-debounce (1200ms — one full breath cycle). The reader approaches presence. The paragraph settles into the focus zone.
dwell-activation.kumbhaka: Related teachings panel is available. The reader may engage or continue reading. No timeout. The dwell state persists until the reader's focus moves.
dwell-activation.recaka: Content transition (300ms). The dwell indicator and related panel recede when focus shifts. Quick and unobtrusive — the reader shouldn't notice the departure.

portal-entry.puraka: Arrival transition (1200ms with 200ms delay, decelerate easing). 'The Opening Moment' (DES-007). The portal pauses before greeting — the slight delay is the indrawn breath before the first word.
portal-entry.kumbhaka: The portal is present. The reader has arrived.
portal-entry.recaka: Browser-native departure. The portal does not animate its own exit.

mode-transitions.to-focus: Content transition (300ms). Chrome retreats. The reading column holds still — the bindu remains while the periphery falls away.
mode-transitions.to-present: Contemplative transition (800ms). Typography scales up. The teaching expands to fill the space.
mode-transitions.to-quiet: Contemplative transition (800ms). Article dims, quiet corner appears. Gentle entry into reflective space.

temporal-rhythm.timing-principle: When uncertain about a transition's duration, ask: 'Is this an approach (pūraka) or a release (recaka)?' Approaches can be brisk (150–300ms) — energy gathering. Releases should be gentle (300–800ms) — energy dissipating. The held state between is always the reader's to determine.

connectivity-states.$description: Visual language for connectivity awareness. CSS alone cannot detect offline state — this is consumer-side implementation guidance. But the design language defines what offline, intermittent, and reconnected states look and feel like. PRI-05 demands that connectivity loss is a normal condition, not an error. A seeker on 2G experiences intermittent connectivity constantly. The design treats this with the same calm as everything else.
connectivity-states.design-stance: Connectivity changes are ambient information, not alerts. The portal does not shout about network status. It quietly adapts — the way a reading lamp dims when the power fluctuates but doesn't flash.

online.$description: Full connectivity. Default state. No indicator needed — absence of any connectivity UI is itself the signal.
online.visual: No connectivity indicator. The design language's normal expression.
online.behavior: All features available. Web fonts load. Search is live.

offline-with-cache.$description: No connectivity, but the seeker has cached content from a previous visit. They can continue reading what they have. This is the most important offline state — the seeker in rural Bihar whose connection dropped mid-chapter should feel no interruption.
offline-with-cache.visual: Subtle ambient indicator — a thin muted bar (text-secondary color at ambient opacity) replacing the gold scroll indicator. Not an error state. The reading experience continues unchanged.
offline-with-cache.behavior: Cached chapters remain readable. Navigation limited to cached content. Search returns cached results with a quiet note. Uncached links show 'Available when connected' in text-secondary, not red error.
offline-with-cache.register: ambient — the seeker should not be pulled out of their reading to notice network status

copy.cached-content: No indicator needed — the content is there, the seeker reads

offline-empty.$description: No connectivity and no cached content. The seeker arrives for the first time without a connection, or their cache is empty. This is the only connectivity state that requires prominent (not urgent) communication.
offline-empty.visual: Centered message in reading typography at instructional register. Gold lotus motif above. No red, no warning icons. The tone is a gentle librarian who says 'the library is closed today, but here is what I can tell you.'

copy.heading: The teachings await your return
copy.body: This portal serves Yogananda's published words. When your connection is available, the complete library will be here.
copy.guidance: If you have visited before, your recent reading may be available in your browser's saved pages.

intermittent.$description: Connection flickering — available, then gone, then back. Common on 2G. The design language's response: do not flicker with it. Debounce visual state changes. Hold the last known state for at least 5 seconds before transitioning to offline. Never oscillate between online and offline indicators.
intermittent.visual: No change during brief dropouts (< 5s). After sustained loss (> 5s), transition to offline-with-cache state using content transition (300ms). When connection returns, gold scroll indicator resumes without announcement.
intermittent.debounce: 5000ms — one full breath cycle before acknowledging connectivity loss
intermittent.behavior: In-flight requests (search, font loading) silently retry once on reconnection. No retry counters, no 'reconnecting...' spinners.

reconnected.$description: Connection restored after an offline period. The design language does not celebrate reconnection — it simply resumes. The teaching was always there; the wire is not the point.
reconnected.visual: Gold scroll indicator returns at ambient opacity. Content transition (300ms). No toast, no banner, no 'You're back online!' — these are marketplace patterns that violate PRI-08.
reconnected.behavior: Background refresh of stale content. Web fonts resume loading if interrupted. Search index updates silently.

consumer-guidance.$description: This section is implementation guidance for consumers (yogananda-teachings, future surfaces). The design system provides the visual language; the consumer implements the detection and state management.
consumer-guidance.service-worker: Register a Service Worker to cache the CSS bundle, current chapter HTML, and critical fonts. The design language's progressive enhancement layers (HTML → CSS → JS) mean cached HTML + CSS produces a complete reading experience.
consumer-guidance.cache-strategy: Cache-first for chapters the seeker has visited. Network-first for search. Stale-while-revalidate for theme CSS and fonts.
consumer-guidance.detection: navigator.onLine for initial state. 'online'/'offline' events for changes. Debounce per the 5000ms rule above.
consumer-guidance.data-attribute: data-connectivity='offline' on <html> when offline. CSS can style based on this. The design system does not define these styles — the consumer does, using the visual language described above.

semantic-html.$description: Expected semantic HTML structure for a reading surface. The design system's CSS targets these elements and attributes. Consumers that use this structure get the full design language expression automatically. This is the contract between the pattern layer and the consumer.

chapter-reader.$description: The complete HTML structure for a chapter reading view. Every element has a semantic role. Every attribute has a purpose. The CSS layer targets these selectors.

structure.purpose: Skip to main content. First focusable element. Visually hidden until focused. DES-025 requirement.
Root. Language, direction, theme, and organization.
ARIA landmark. Contains the chapter content. Target of skip-nav.
Self-contained chapter. Screen readers announce article boundaries.
Chapter header: label, title, epigraph.
Chapter title. Single h1 per page, or h2 if page has a site-level h1.
Each paragraph. data-paragraph enables dwell, keyboard navigation, and golden thread targeting.
First letter of first paragraph. Explicit span, not ::first-letter (avoids superscript footnote collision).
Chapter opening quotation.
Scene break within chapter. Decorative — hidden from screen readers.
Book photograph or illustration.
Figure caption. Always present for accessibility.
Inline footnote reference. DPUB-ARIA.
Chapter endnotes section. DPUB-ARIA.
Return link from endnote to reference point. DPUB-ARIA.
Reading content container. Receives hanging-punctuation, touch padding, hover effects.
Publication overlay activation. When present, structural elements shift to crimson.
Reader mode. Controls chrome visibility and typography scaling.
Dwell mode active. Dims non-target paragraphs.

passage-card.$description: Semantic structure for a standalone passage display (search results, Today's Wisdom).

structure.purpose: Self-contained passage.
The passage text. Sacred register.
Full attribution. PRI-02.
'Read in context' link. PRI-02 — every passage links to its full chapter.
